observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
a. CGSO to prepare and submit the RPCPPE in the prescribed form, wherein the PPE
were classified per category or type to facilitate reconciliation with the accounting
records; and
b. City Accountant and CGSO to intensify the review and reconciliation of PPELC
and PC through in-depth verification and analysis of PPE accounts and identify
the cause/s of noted deficiencies, and effect necessary adjustments in their
respective records.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
• City Treasurer to prepare a Schedule of Unreleased Checks at the end of the year
and submit it to the City Accountant: and
• City Accountant, on the basis of the report from the City Treasurer, to prepare the
JEV for the restoration of cash equivalent of the unreleased checks and recognize
the appropriate payable/liability accounts and in addition, reverse the entries made
in the ensuing year, in compliance with the pertinent provisions of COA GAFMIS
Circular Letter No. 2002-001.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
a. submit to the Audit Team all necessary documents such as Delivery Receipts,
Acknowledgement Receipts, Contractor’s Statement of Work Accomplished,
Certificate of Inspection and Acceptance and other pertinent documents to prove
the validity of the recorded Accounts Payable, otherwise make the necessary
adjustments in the books of accounts; and
b. refrain from recording Accounts Payable merely on the basis of Obligation
Requests, and instead, ensure that all relevant supporting documents are
attached to the claims.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer to furnish the City
Accountant, at the beginning of the year, a duly certified list showing the name of
taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year and the City Accountant to
use this as his basis in preparing a Journal Entry Voucher to set-up Real Property Tax
Receivable/Special Education Tax Receivable and the corresponding Deferred Real
Property Tax Income/Deferred Special Education Tax Income.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to:
a. cause the immediate reversion to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund
of the Accounts Payable totaling P21,340,569.09 which remained outstanding for
more than two years and were not supported with complete documentation; and
b. inform in writing the concerned suppliers and contractors about their unclaimed
retention money with the advice that the amount will be reverted to the
unappropriated surplus of the general fund of the City should proper claim be not
made within reasonable period.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and City Treasurer
to continue their ongoing efforts to determine the origin and details of the reported
discrepancy between the reciprocal accounts Due to and Due from Other Funds.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to refrain from
granting additional cash advances to any official, officer or employee without
liquidating first his/her prior cash advances, and to instead designate a Special
Disbursing Officer for the grant of additional cash advances for special
activities/projects.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to increase the
amount of fidelity bond of the subject AOs to cover the large amount of their
CAs pursuant to Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 to protect the interest of the City
Government, or otherwise, limit the amount of their cash advances up to the
maximum cash accountability of their fidelity bond.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer and City Accountant
to propose specific and achievable measures to resolve delays in the liquidation of
CAs, which should include, among others, the City Accountant’s regular sending of
demand letters to AOs with a reminder that their salaries could be withheld should
they still fail to liquidate after demand.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the CGSO to expedite the disposal of the
unserviceable property by submitting to the COA Auditor the request for disposal of the
unserviceable properties together with the IIRUP, Appraisal Report and other
supporting documents.

observations.managementAction: PPE Ledger Cards indicating the details of the PPE of the City were maintained by the Accounting Office. Property Cards were updated according to the PPE’s classification. Continuous reconciliation of Accounting and GSO records.
observations.reason: Discrepancy amounting to P387,673,486.20 still existed between the records of the Accounting and GSO. The submitted RPCPPE were still not in the prescribed form and not grouped according to type or category of assets.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to exert effort to retrieve records and analyze the composition of Construction in Progress–Buildings and Other Structures account with year-end balance of P124,498,646.53 and make the appropriate adjustments on the books, if necessary.
observations.managementAction: Retrieval of records to determine the composition of the Construction in Progress–Buildings and Other Structures account with year-end balance of P124,498,646.53 is ongoing. JEV No. G-21-03-05 dated March 31, 2021 was drawn to adjust the Construction in Progress–Buildings and other Structures amounting to P40,964,168.29. For the remaining amount, the City Accounting Office is still on the process of retrieving relative records in order to determine the aforementioned account.
observations.reason: For the remaining amount, the City Accounting Office is still in the process of determining the composition of the aforementioned account.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to: a. Retrieve records pertaining to the acquisition of the Public Infrastructures totalling P661,059,371.94; b. Prepare and maintain Local Road Networks Ledger Cards (LRNLC) and Other Public Infrastructure Ledger Cards (OPILC) for the aforementioned accounts; and c. Compute and journalize the depreciation of the subject assets from the time of acquisition to present.
observations.managementAction: Records pertaining to the acquisition of the Public Infrastructures were retrieved by the City Accountant. LRNLC and OPILC were prepared and maintained by the City Accountant. JEV No. G-21-09-32 dated September 30, 2021 was drawn to record the depreciation of the aforementioned PPEs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to draw JEV to adjust the overstatement of Other Supplies and Materials Inventory and Government Equity accounts. We also recommended that the City Accountant be consistent in recording inventory items by treating as Assets upon acquisition and to recognize the Expense portion upon issuances of Inventories.
observations.managementAction: JEV No. S-21-03-02 dated March 12, 2021 was drawn to adjust the overstatement of the subject accounts. Proper recording of receipt and issuance of inventory items by treating inventory items as Assets upon acquisition and recognition of Expense upon issuance were observed by the City Accountant.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Budget Officer, Accountant and Treasurer exercise diligence, prudence and proper control in the disposition of government funds by limiting the expenditures within the total income realized during the year to avoid incurrence of cash overdraft. They should take concrete and effective measures to cover the cash deficit with the collections in the ensuing year.
observations.managementAction: Constant review and analysis of income and expenditures of the City was conducted to ensure that no cash deficit will be incurred. Measures were implemented to avoid cash deficit.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to coordinate with the responsible officials to facilitate the immediate submission to the Commission on Audit of the lacking documents and/or correction of deficiencies noted in audit to prevent suspension and/or disallowance of the transactions. Henceforth, ensure that all required supporting documents are attached to the Disbursement Vouchers (DVs) before payment to facilitate the review of the validity and propriety of the disbursement.
observations.managementAction: Required supporting documents were attached to the DVs of the City.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the: a. City Accountant to properly record the purchases of welfare goods under the account Welfare Goods for Distribution and the distributed portion to Welfare Goods Expenses. He should also maintain Supplies Ledger Cards for all welfare goods; and b. City Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer (CDRRMO)/GSO Officer to maintain Stock Cards to record the purchases and issuances to report monthly RSMI to the Accounting Office as basis of the City Accountant in recording issued items under account Welfare Goods Expenses.
observations.managementAction: Purchase and distribution of welfare goods were properly recorded. Supplies Ledger Cards for welfare goods were maintained by the City Accountant. Stock Cards were maintained and RSMI were submitted monthly to the City Accountant by the CDRRMO.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to draw JEV to adjust the understatement in the Due to LGUs account and the overstatement of the Government Equity both in General Fund and SEF. We also recommended that the City Accountant properly recognize the provincial share from the collections of basic real property and special education taxes and penalties thereon, which had become due prior to its cityhood, in the books of accounts using the account Due to LGUs and maintain the corresponding subsidiary ledger for the Provincial account.
observations.managementAction: Adjustments were made through JEV No. G-21-02-33 dated February 28, 2021 for the General Fund and JEV No. S-21-02-04 dated February 5, 2021 for the SEF. Proper recognition of the Provincial share from the collections of property taxes were recognized through JEV No. G-21-03-06 dated March 3, 2021 for the General Fund and JEV No. S-21-03-03 dated March 3, 2021 for the SEF.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the OIC-City Treasurer to adopt the following suggested measures: a. Determine the running balance of the cashbook, record the transactions daily and reconcile the balance with the actual cash on hand for deposit at the end of each day; b. Close the cashbooks at the end of the month by double-ruling below the last entries and accomplish the prescribed certification; and c. Reconcile the cashbook balance with the accounting records and to take up immediately the necessary corrections in the books requiring adjustments.
observations.managementAction: Daily transactions were recorded in the cashbooks. The cashbook and accounting records were reconciled. Prescribed certification was provided in some months.
observations.reason: Running balances for several months were not closed and ruled.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the GSO to expedite the disposal of the unserviceable motor vehicles with total carrying value of P3,403,409.10 in accordance with the aforementioned regulations and upon disposal, the City Accountant should draw a JEV to drop from the books the cost of the unserviceable motor vehicles.
observations.managementAction: Unserviceable motor vehicles with total carrying value of P3,403,409.10 were disposed in accordance with the aforementioned regulations. Upon disposal, the cost of unserviceable motor vehicles disposed were dropped from the books of the City thru JEV No. G-21-06-15.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and the General
Services Officer to take the following specific actions or measures to address the
discrepancy of P444,632,007.23 between the accounting records and inventory report,
to wit:

a. the Accounting Office update and maintain the PPE Ledger Cards indicating
therein the details of every property acquired by the City, such as the
acquisition, description, custody, estimated useful life, depreciation, impairment
loss, disposal and other information about the asset;
b. the General Services Office determine the composition of Land Improvements
reported in lump-sum, report the results of inventory taking for buildings,
structures and public infrastructures in the prescribed RPCPPE and to group the
physically counted PPE according to type or category of assets;
c. the General Services Office update and maintain the Property Cards according
to account classification; and
d. the Accounting and General Services Offices reconcile their respective PPE
records on a regular basis.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to exert effort to
retrieve records and analyze the composition and details of Construction in Progress-
Buildings and Other Structure account with year-end balance of P124,498,646.53 and
make the appropriate adjustments on the books, if necessary.

We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:

a. retrieve records pertaining to the acquisition of the Public Infrastructures
totalling P661,059,371.94;
b. prepare and maintain the required LRNLC and OPILC for the aforementioned
accounts; and
c. compute and journalize the depreciation of the subject assets from the time of
acquisition to present.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to draw Journal
Voucher to adjust the overstatement of Other Supplies and Materials Inventory and
Government Equity accounts.

We also recommended that the City Accountant be consistent in recording Inventory
items by treating these as Assets upon acquisition and to recognize the Expense
portion upon the issuances of Inventories.

We recommended that the City Budget Officer, Accountant and Treasurer exercise
diligence, prudence and proper control in the disposition of government funds by
limiting the expenditures within the total income realized during the year to avoid
incurrence of cash overdraft. It is likewise recommended that they take concrete and
effective measures to cover the cash deficit with the collections in the ensuing year.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to coordinate with
responsible officials to facilitate the immediate submission to the Commission on Audit
of the lacking documents and/or correction of deficiencies noted in audit to prevent
suspension and/or disallowance of the transactions. Henceforth, ensure that all
required supporting documents are attached to the DVs before payment to
facilitate the review of the validity and propriety of the disbursement.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. City Accountant to properly record the purchases of welfare goods under the
account Welfare Goods for Distribution and the distributed portion to Welfare
Goods Expenses. He should also maintain Supplies Ledger Cards for all welfare
goods; and
b. City Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer/City General Services Officer to
maintain Stock Cards to record the purchases and issuances of welfare goods.
The issuances of these items should be supported with the RIS and reported monthly to
the Accounting Office thru the preparation and submission of monthly RSMI as basis of
the City Accountant in recording issued items under account Welfare Goods
Expenses.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to draw Journal
Voucher to adjust the understatement in Due to LGUs account and the overstatement
of the Government Equity both in General and Special Education Funds.

We also recommended that the City Accountant properly recognize the provincial
share from the collections of basic real property and special education taxes and
penalties thereon, which had become past due prior to its cityhood, in the books of
accounts using the account Due to LGUs and maintain the corresponding subsidiary
ledger for the Provincial account.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the OIC, City Treasurer to properly
maintain the cashbooks by adopting the following suggested measures:

a. Determine the running balance of the cashbook, record transactions daily and
reconcile the balance with the actual cash on hand for deposit at the end of each
day;
b. Close the cashbooks at the end of the month by double-ruling below the last
entries and accomplish the prescribed certification; and
c. Reconcile the cashbook balance with the accounting records and to take up
immediately the necessary corrections in the books requiring adjustments.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the General Services Office (GSO) to
expedite the disposal of the unserviceable motor vehicles with total carrying value of
P3,403,409.10 in accordance with the aforementioned regulations to halt further
deterioration and reduction of their economic values.

Upon disposal, the City Accountant should draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to drop
from the books of accounts the cost of the unserviceable motor vehicles.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct:
a. the Inventory Committee to conduct a comprehensive physical count of all properties of the City;
b. the General Services Officer (GSO) to see to it that all PPEs are covered with Property Acknowledgement Receipt which should be regularly renewed and/or cancelled every time there is change in the end-user of the PPE; and
c. the GSO to maintain Property Cards for individual property items owned by the City for reconciliation with accounting records and control purposes.
observations.managementAction:
The Inventory Committee conducted a comprehensive physical count of all the City.

The GSO tagged the physically counted PPE and updated the issuance of PAR.

GSO had started updating the Property Cards.
observations.reason: Details of the property acquired in later part of 2020 were for uploading in the computer system being installed in the GSO.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
• retrieve records pertaining to the acquisition of the Public Infrastructures totalling P661,059,371.94;
• prepare and maintain the required PPELC for the aforementioned accounts; and
• compute and journalize the depreciation of the subject assets from the time of acquisition up to the present.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office had started retrieving records pertaining to Public Infrastructures.
observations.reason: The City Accounting Office has inadequate records pertaining to acquisition of PPE prior to 2010. Not PPELC is yet to be maintained after retrieving all the pertinent records.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:
• create an Inventory Committee that shall conduct the semi-annual physical count of all inventory items, prepare the required RPCI and submit the same to the Auditor concerned not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively;
• require the concerned personnel to reconcile the results of the Physical Count with the records kept by the respective Offices and Accounting records; and
• require the concerned offices to take up necessary adjustments in the books for the discrepancy/ies noted in the reconciliation.
observations.managementAction:
Physical count by Offices handling the inventory items was conducted and the corresponding report was submitted to COA.

The balances in the RPCI tally with the Accounting records.

Periodic reconciliation and adjustments were being undertaken by the concerned offices.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Local Finance Committee to analyze the trend in the collection of tax revenues for the last three years and use the results of the analysis to come up with estimates of income with reasonable assurance of collectibility to facilitate the preparation of a realistic Annual Budget for the ensuing fiscal year.
observations.managementAction:
Collection trend was being analyzed in the preparation of budget in the ensuing year.

Although the City had not realized its targeted income for 2020 due to COVID 19 pandemic, they minimized their expenditures in the General Fund to come up with adequate cash available for operation at year-end.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
• City Accountant to properly record the purchases of supplies, materials, equipment and relief goods under the account Welfare Goods for Distribution and the distributed portion to Welfare Goods Expenses. The City Accountant should also maintain Supplies Ledger Cards for all welfare goods; and
• City Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer/General Services Officer to maintain Stock Cards to record the purchases and issuances of welfare goods. The issuances of these items should be supported with the Requisition Issue Slip (RIS) and reported monthly to the Accounting Office thru the preparation and submission of monthly Report of Supplies and Materials Issued (RSMI) as basis of the City Accountant in recording issued items under account Welfare Goods Expenses.
observations.managementAction:
Relief goods purchased out of the LDRRMF were recorded under Other Supplies and Materials Expense and the distributed portion to Other Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses.

Stock cards for relief goods treated as inventory items were maintained.

There were inconsistencies in recording goods.
observations.reason: Relief goods purchased out of the LDRRMF were recorded under Other Supplies and Materials Expense and Other Maintenance and Operating Expenses. There were inconsistencies in recording goods.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and the CSWDO to again send demand letters to the debtors as their capacity to pay and/or economic circumstances may have possibly improved through the years and to seek the advice of the City Legal Office on various alternative legal remedies that may be pursued by the City in order to enforce the collection of the receivables.
observations.managementAction: The City government and CSWDO actions are geared towards requesting for write-off of the long outstanding Loans Receivables after efforts exerted in collecting these accounts were found futile.
observations.reason: The recipients of the loans receivables under the SEA program of the City were not able to pay their outstanding loans despite all the efforts exerted by the CSWDO.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
• exert extra effort to analyze and determine the details of the Due to BIR account to come up with reliable SLs in all Funds and for each type of tax being withheld and remitted; and
• ensure that all taxes withheld are accordingly remitted within the prescribed period in compliance with BIR Revenue Memorandum Circular 23-2012 dated February 14, 2012 to prevent the incurrence of surcharges and penalties.
observations.managementAction:
The City Accountant had started analyzing the details of the Due to BIR account to come up with reliable SLs for the account.

The City Accountant warrants that all taxes withheld during the year were remitted within the prescribed period.
observations.reason: Alternative work arrangement being implemented in City Accounting Office affected the availability of manpower and its accomplishments.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and the City Human Resource Management Officer (CHRMO) to exert effort to analyze and determine the details of the unremitted Due to GSIS and cause its immediate remittance to the GSIS.

We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and the CHRMO to strictly comply with Republic Act No. 8291 and ensure that all deductions from employees' salaries and the corresponding government share due to the System are fully remitted within the prescribed period.
observations.managementAction:
The City Accountant and CHRMO determined the composition of the unremitted Due to GSIS and remitted the same to the concerned Agency.

The City Accountant warrants that all deductions from employees' salaries and the corresponding government share are remitted within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and the City Human Resource Management Officer (CHRMO) to analyze, trace and determine the details of the unremitted Due to Pag-IBIG and cause its immediate remittance to the HDMF.

We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and the CHRMO to comply with Republic Act No. 9679 and HDMF Circular No. 275 by ensuring that the amount of employer and employees' shares are remitted in full within the prescribed period to avoid payment of penalties for the late remittance.
observations.managementAction: The CHRMO and the City Accountant implemented measures and ensured that the amount of employer and employees' shares are remitted in full within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the General Services Office (GSO) to expedite the disposal of the unserviceable office furniture, fixtures, equipment and motor vehicles in accordance with the aforementioned regulations and thereafter require the City Accountant to draw the Journal Entry Voucher to drop from the books the cost of the unserviceable fixed assets.
observations.managementAction:
Disposal of unserviceable office furniture, fixtures and equipment was yet to be undertaken by the City in CY 2020. Preparation of the needed documents to support the disposal is still on-going.

Thereafter, it is recommended that the City Accountant draw the Journal Entry Voucher to drop from the books the cost of the unserviceable fixed assets.

Journal Entry Vouchers Nos. G20-12-10 and G20-12-11 were drawn to record the dropping of the books of the disposed office furniture, fixtures and equipment.
observations.reason: Preparation of the needed documents to support the disposal is still on-going.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and the City Welfare and Development Officer to facilitate the submission to the COA of the request for authority to write-off the dormant receivables amounting to P1,645,559.00, together with the copies of the demand letters duly sent and/or received by concerned grantees.
observations.managementAction: Actions will be taken by Management towards reminding the debtors of their obligation to the City Government.
observations.reason: Actions were not taken towards requesting authority from COA to write-off the dormant receivables amounting to P1,645,559.00.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct:

a. the Inventory Committee to conduct a comprehensive physical inventory of all
assets of the City to determine their actual existence and condition and
accordingly prepare the corresponding RPCPPE;
b. the General Services Officer (GSO) to see to it that all PPEs are covered with
Property Acknowledgement Receipt which should be regularly renewed and/or
cancelled every time there is change in the end-user of the PPE; and
c. the GSO to maintain Property Cards for individual property items owned by
the City for reconciliation with accounting records and control purposes.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:

• retrieve records pertaining to the acquisition of the Public Infrastructures
totalling P661,059,371.94;
• prepare and maintain the PPELC for the aforementioned accounts; and
• compute and journalize the depreciation of the subject assets from the time of
acquisition up to the present.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

• create an Inventory Committee that shall conduct the semi-annual physical count
of all inventory items, prepare the required RPCI and submit the same to the Auditor
concerned not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and
second semesters, respectively;
• require the concerned personnel to reconcile the results of the Physical Count with
the records kept by the respective Offices and Accounting records; and
• require the concerned offices to take up necessary adjustments in the books for
the discrepancy/ies noted in the reconciliation.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Local Finance Committee to analyze
the trend in the collection of tax revenues for the last three years and use the results
of the analysis to come up with estimates of income with reasonable assurance of
collectibility to facilitate the preparation of a realistic Annual Budget for the ensuing
fiscal year.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

• City Accountant to properly record the purchases of supplies, materials,
equipment and relief goods under the account Welfare Goods for Distribution and the distributed
portion to Welfare Goods Expenses.
• City Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer/General Services Officer to
maintain Stock Cards to record the purchases and issuances of supplies,
materials and relief goods procured out of LDRRMF. Issuances of these items
should be supported with RIS and reported monthly to the Accounting Office thru
the preparation and submission of monthly RSMI; and
• City Accountant, City Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer and the
General Services Officer to comply with the accounting and reporting guidelines
on the utilization of the LDRRMF prescribed under COA Circular Nos. 2014-002 and
2015-009 dated April 15, 2014 and December 1, 2015, respectively, to ensure the
correctness and reliability of the financial reporting of the City.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and the CSWDO to
again send demand letters to the debtors as their capacity to pay and/or economic
circumstances may have possibly improved through the years and to seek the advice of
the City Legal Office on various alternative legal remedies that may be pursued by the
City in order to enforce the collection of the receivables.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:

• exert extra effort to analyze and determine the details of the Due to BIR account
to come up with reliable SLs in all Funds and for each type of tax being withheld
and remitted by the City; and
• ensure that all taxes withheld are accordingly remitted within the prescribed
period in compliance with BIR Revenue Memorandum Circular 23-2012 dated
February 14, 2012 to prevent the incurrence of surcharges and penalties.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and the City Human
Resource Management Officer to exert effort to analyze and determine the composition
of the unremitted Due to GSIS and cause its immediate remittance to the GSIS.

We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and the City
Human Resource Management Officer to ensure that all deductions from employees’
salaries and the corresponding government share due to the System are fully remitted
within the prescribed period in compliance with GSIS Act of 1997.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and the City Human
Resource Management Officer (CHRMO) to exert effort to analyze, trace and determine
the details of the unremitted Due to Pag-IBIG and cause its immediate remittance to the
HDMF.

We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and the CHRMO
to strictly comply with Republic Act No. 9679 and HDMF Circular No. 275 by ensuring
that the amount of employer and employees’ shares are remitted in full within the
prescribed period to avoid payment of penalties for the late remittance.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the General Services Office (GSO) to
expedite the disposal of the unserviceable office furniture and fixtures, equipment and
motor vehicles with total carrying value of P1,838,666.91 in accordance with the
aforementioned regulations.

Thereafter, it is recommended that the City Accountant draw a Journal Entry Voucher
(JEV) to drop from the books the cost of the unserviceable fixed assets.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and the City General Services Officer to continue exerting efforts to analyze and reconcile the discrepancy of P128,469,835.46 between the accounting records and the RPCPPE.
observations.managementAction: Reconciliation of records is being undertaken by the Offices of General Services and Accounting.
observations.reason: Inadequate records pertaining to acquisition of PPE prior to 2010 hinders the reconciliation of records.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to analyze and investigate the cause/s of the net deficiency of P358,719,513.19 between the balances of the RPT and SET Receivables and the Certified List of Delinquent Taxes as of December 31, 2018, and make the necessary adjustments in their records.
observations.managementAction: The cleansing of real property records/assessment, tax collection, recording and accounting processes were being fast-tracked to get rid of duplication in assessment. Notices of Cancellation had been issued for properties with new declarant copy furnished the Treasurer’s Office for their record and information. The difference between the Receivables and Certified List of Collectible Tax Delinquency was reduced to P45,936,097.60 as of December 31, 2019.
observations.reason: Absence of accounting records for individual taxpayers to support the RPT/SET Receivables hinders the reconciliation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to ensure that all disbursements are sufficiently backed up with available cash by preparing the Daily Cash Position Report as her guide and to adopt strategies and measures to increase revenues/collections. We also recommended that the City Mayor require the City Budget Officer to ensure that all disbursements/expenditures are within the approved budget and total income realized and collected during the year to avoid incurrence of cash overdraft.
observations.managementAction: The City Budget Officer always regularly monitoring the availability of funds ensuring that all obligations have cash backed up.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to write Letters of Demand to Accountable Officers with unsettled cash advances beyond 60 days and henceforth strictly monitor settlement of cash advances. We also recommended that no additional cash advances shall be granted to employees qualified to draw cash advance unless his or her previous cash advance/s are fully settled.
observations.managementAction: Monitoring of unliquidated cash advances was being undertaken by the Accounting Office.
observations.reason: Additional cash advances were not granted to employees with outstanding cash advance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to install effective controls to ensure correct computation of penalties or discounts in the payment of taxes.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer had instructed the system provider to see to it that penalties or discounts are correctly computed by the system.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and other concerned officials to strictly comply with BIR Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 23-2012 by ensuring that the amount of all taxes withheld are remitted in full on or before the 10th day of the following month. We likewise recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to exert extra effort to analyze, trace and determine the details of the Due to BIR account amounting to P3,527,755.84 and cause its immediate remittance to the BIR and/or make the necessary adjustments in the City’s books of accounts, as the case maybe.
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office had started analyzing the details of the Due to BIR.
observations.reason: Lack of reliable Subsidiary Ledgers for Due to BIR account hindered determination of the composition of the unremitted balance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and the City Human Resource Management Officer to strictly comply with PhilHealth Circular No. 0004, series of 2013 by ensuring that the amount of employer’s and employees’ shares are remitted in full to the PhilHealth on or before the 10th calendar day of the month following the applicable month. We likewise recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to exert extra effort to analyze, trace and determine the details of the Due to PhilHealth amounting to P260,961.68 and cause its immediate remittance to PhilHealth and/or make the necessary adjustments in the City’s books of accounts as the case maybe.
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office analyzed the details of the Due to PhilHealth and necessary adjustment was taken up in the books.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Head of City Social Welfare and Development Office (CSWDO) to submit to the Audit Team the names or lists of day care centers and children who benefited from the SFP and proof, such as Certificate of Acceptance or equivalent document from the participating teachers, parents or caregivers, that the food supplies or meals were served or distributed to the beneficiaries, to prevent suspension of the amount of P7,139,630.50 in audit.
observations.managementAction: Copy of the master list of beneficiaries and other documents to support the disbursements for the Supplemental Feeding Program was submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the GAD Focal Person to provide additional valid explanation to justify the inclusion of the construction of various facilities and infrastructure projects costing P167,080,575.39 in the City’s GAD Plan and Budget and/or Accomplishment Reports. Otherwise, we recommended that the cost of the projects be excluded from the computation or assessment of the City’s compliance with the 5 percent GAD threshold. Henceforth, we recommended that the City Mayor instruct the GAD Focal Person to ensure that only programs, projects and activities with unequivocal relevance to GAD are included in the GPB and Accomplishment Reports.
observations.managementAction: The GAD Focal Person explained that the most of the marginalized sector in the City live in the slums and with no facilities and road accessibility, thus the construction of multi-purpose hall and other infrastructure projects were identified as part of the Program/Plan/Activities (PPAs) to address the gender issue. The GAD Focal Person ensures that PPAs included in the GAD Plan and Budget had great importance to constituents and will benefit all sectors particularly women and children.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Budget Officer, the City Accountant, the Head of the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office to refrain from utilizing the unexpended balances of the LDRRMF unless there is an approved revised or supplemental LDRMM Plan specifying the programs and activities to be funded by the amount. We also recommended that the City Mayor instruct the concerned officials to refrain from utilizing the unexpended balances of the LDRRMF unless there is an approved revised or supplemental LDRMM Plan specifying the programs and activities to be funded by the amount.
observations.managementAction: The City adhered to the audit recommendation by ensuring that utilization of the unexpended balances of LDRRMF in Trust Fund were covered with SP Resolution and included in the Revised LDDRM Plan.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and other concerned City officials to exert extra efforts to locate old records, more particularly from the years 2015 and 2016, and/or make representation with the Department of Budget and Management for possible assistance in determining the source or origin and details of the Due to NGAs balance of P548,474.04 to facilitate the immediate return of the amount to the source agency in accordance with Item 6.7 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994.
observations.managementAction: Collection of Fire Fees in previous years which were remitted to Bureau of Treasury on June 28, 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Health Officer and/or other requisitioning and medical personnel to ensure that every Purchase Request for drugs and medicines shall be accompanied by a certification signed by the requisitioning officer that the drug products being requisitioned fall within and conform with PNDF, Volume I as required under EO No. 49 dated January 21, 1993 and DOH Administrative Order No. 2012-0023-A dated December 26, 2013.
observations.managementAction: The City Health Office conformed with the guidelines of EO No. 49 dated January 21, 1993 and DOH Administrative Order No. 2012-0023-A in the procurement of drugs and medicines.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Assessor to fast-track the Tax Mapping operations to facilitate the cleansing of the Real Property Tax system in coordination with the Offices of the Assessor and Treasurer to get rid of the duplication in assessment of real property units and to adjust accordingly the reported tax delinquencies. Consequently, direct the City Assessor to specify the specific address/location of the properties to effect remedial measures to collect tax delinquencies.
observations.managementAction: Tax mapping operation of the City was fast-tracked. Duplication noted were cancelled.
observations.reason: Tax mapping operation is still ongoing and duplication noted were in the process of being cancelled.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Health Officer and/or other requisitioning medical personnel to ensure that every Purchase Request for drugs and medicines shall be accompanied by a certification signed by the requisitioning officer that the drug products being requisitioned fall within and conform with PNDF, Volume I as required under EO No. 49 dated January 21, 1993 and DOH Administrative Order No. 2012-0023-A dated December 26, 2013.
observations.managementAction: Not Concerned Implemented officials did not provide clear explanation on reason of their non-com- pliance with the audit recommendation.
observations.reason: Concerned officials did not provide clear explanation on reason of their non-compliance with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor direct:
a. the General Services Officer and the created appraisal and disposal commit- tee to facilitate the immediate disposal of all unserviceable properties in order to avail of a higher economic value when offered in public auction;
b. the concerned officials to strictly comply with the policies and procedures on the disposal of property provided in COA Circular No. 92-386. Upon disposal, the Accounting Office should prepare a Journal Entry Voucher to drop from the books of accounts the sold/condemned properties; and
c. the City Accountant to reclassify the unserviceable PPE accounts to Other Assets account.
observations.managementAction:
Not Concerned Implemented officials did not provide clear ex- planation on the reason of their non-compliance with the audit recommenda- tion.
Not Concerned Implemented officials did not provide clear ex- planation on the reason of their non-compliance with the audit recommenda- tion.
Not Concerned Implemented officials did not provide clear ex- planation on the reason of their non-compliance with the audit recom- menda- tion.
observations.reason: Concerned officials did not provide clear explanation on the reason of their non-compliance with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and the City
General Services Officer to continue exerting efforts to analyze and reconcile the
discrepancy of P128,469,835.46 between the accounting records and the RPCPPE.

Management’s Comment:
Though the variance had been decreasing every year, the City GSO Officer and the City
Accountant assured that they will continue the process of reconciliation of their records.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct the City Assessor
and the City Treasurer to fast track the completion of the tax mapping
activities/operations that will facilitate the cleansing and improvement of the
City’s real property assessment, tax collection, recording and accounting
processes.

We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and the City
Treasurer to analyze and investigate the cause/s of the net deficiency of
P358,719,513.19 between the balances of the RPT and SET Receivables and the
Certified List of Delinquent Taxes as of December 31, 2018, and make the
necessary adjustments in their records.

Management’s Comment:
The City Treasurer and the City Assessor agree to fast track the full implementation of
tax mapping system to address the problem. The former and the City Accountant
likewise agreed to reconcile their records.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to ensure that all
disbursements are sufficiently backed up with available cash by preparing the
Daily Cash Position Report as her guide and to adopt strategies and measures to
increase revenues/collections.

We also recommended that the City Mayor require the City Budget Officer to
ensure that all disbursements/expenditures are within the approved budget and
total income realized and collected during the year to avoid incurrence of cash
overdraft.

Management’s Comment:
The Audit Team was informed by concerned officials during the exit conference that the
variance was due to late reconciliation between the Treasurer’s Office and the
Accounting Office. They were already in the process of using a system that will provide
real time results to lessen the variance.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
The Audit Team did not observe that there was delay in the reconciliation of records
between the Treasurer’s Office and the Accounting Office.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to write Letters
of Demand to Accountable Officers with unsettled cash advances beyond 60 days
and henceforth strictly monitor settlement of cash advances.

We also recommended that no additional cash advances shall be granted to
employees qualified to draw cash advance unless his or her previous cash
advance/s are fully settled.

Management’s Comment:
The City Accountant confirmed that they already had a designated staff to monitor
whether an officer or employee has an outstanding cash advance before granting
additional cash advance.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to issue notice to
concerned taxpayers requesting for additional payments to settle the unpaid
interests on delinquent real property taxes or refund the excess discounts given,
to prevent the issuance by the Audit Team of Notice of Charge against concerned
Collecting Officers in the amount of P1,517,481.50.

Henceforth, we recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to
install effective controls to ensure correct imposition or computation of penalties
or discounts in the payment of taxes.

Management’s Comment:
Concerned officials stated that the Molino Doctors and SM Prime Holdings’ assessments
were for new machinery and promised to formally submit relevant documents. The City
Treasurer also explained that after they had received the AOM, they tested the
computerized system and determined the errors in the computation of penalties. They
promised to act on the problem.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and other
concerned officials to strictly comply with BIR Revenue Memorandum Circular No.
23-2012 by ensuring that the amount of all taxes withheld are remitted in full on or
before the 10th day of the following month.

We likewise recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to
exert extra effort to analyze, trace and determine the details of the Due to BIR
account amounting to P3,527,755.84 and cause its immediate remittance to the
BIR and/or make the necessary adjustments in the City’s books of accounts, as
the case maybe.

Management’s Comment:
Concerned officials agreed to comply with the Audit Team’s recommendations.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and the City
Human Resource Management Officer to strictly comply with PhilHealth Circular
No. 0004, series of 2013 by ensuring that the amount of employer’s and
employees’ shares are remitted in full to the PhilHealth on or before the 10th
calendar day of the month following the applicable month to which the payment is
due and applicable.

We likewise recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to
exert extra effort to analyze, trace and determine the details of the Due to
PhilHealth account amounting to P260,961.68 and cause its immediate remittance
to the PhilHealth and/or make the necessary adjustments in the City’s books of
accounts as the case maybe.

Management Comment’s:
Concerned officials promised to look into the deficiencies noted in audit.

observations.managementAction:
The Inventory Committee has already completed the inventory count of all properties owned by the City.
The GSO had provided details in the PPE Ledger Cards and made sure that the PPELC are updated.
The GSO ensured that Property Cards are properly maintained and updated.
Reconciliaton of PPE records is continuously being done, resulting in the decrease in the noted discrepancy.
IIRUP had been prepared and submitted.
observations.reason: Concerned officials did not provide expla- nation on their partial implementation of the audit recommenda- tion.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant observe PPSAS 17 and appropriate PPE accounts, initiate efforts towards recognition in the books using cost or appraised value of unrecorded PPE for fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant had booked the subject PPE under Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) No. G-18-03-17 dated March 31, 2018 and recognized them in the books at cost pursuant to the PPSAS (now International PSAS) No. 17.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor require:
a. the General Services Officer (GSO) to consolidate weekly Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) using the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) and submit to the Accounting Office for recording;
b. the City Accountant to compute the cost of supplies issued and ending inventory based on the SSMI and to prepare a JEV to record expenditures using appropriate expenditures account; and
c. the GSO to conduct a complete physical count of all inventory items regularly at the end of every semester and render a report thereon within the prescribed dead-line and reconcile the records of the GSO with the Accounting re-cords on a per category basis.
observations.managementAction:
The GSO has been making weekly consolidation of the RIS using the SSMI and submit it to the Accounting for proper recording.
The City Accountant had drawn JEV No. S-18-02-05 dated Feb. 28, 2018 to record the appropriate expenditure account/s.
Complete physical count of all inventory items was being done every semester.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to closely monitor the cash balance of each fund and enjoin the City Treasurer from certifying funds availability when no cash is available to avoid cash deficits. Otherwise, appropriate sanctions maybe imposed against responsible officials for existence of cash deficits.
We also recommended that trust funds be utilized only for the specific purpose for which it was intended as required under Section 4(3) of PD No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: Management ensured that trust funds had been utilized according to the purpose/s for which they were given.
observations.reason: Management was still in the process of implementing effective cash management.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the fast-tracking of the Tax Mapping operations to facilitate the cleansing of the Real Property Tax system in coordination with the Offices of the Assessor and Treasurer to get rid of the tax duplication in assessment of real property units and to adjust accordingly the reported tax delinquencies. Consequently, direct the City Assessor to specify the specific address/location of the properties to effect remedial measures to collect tax delinquencies.
observations.managementAction: The City Assessor has a pending request from the Land Registration Authority for copies of titled lots in the City, a way to start the tax mapping process.

Reconciliation has started and expected to be completed in few more months.
Registration process of vehicles under the names of previous owners is still ongoing.
A follow-up and coordination with LTO had already been made.
Annual registration of vehicles is being observed.
Subject IIRUP had been prepared by the GSO.
observations.reason: Concerned officials did not provide explanation on their partial or partial implementation of the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
a. submit immediately the 17 DVs covering disbursements amounting to P3,088,807.90, otherwise the Audit Team will be constrained to issue a Notice of Suspension for non-submission thereof; and
b. ensure that submission of financial reports/ Disbursement Vouchers together with the required supporting documents thereto conform strictly with the deadline set in period of Section 7.2.1(a) of the RRSA prescribed in COA Circular No. 2009-006.
observations.managementAction:
The subject DVs had been submitted on April 4, 2018.
No delay in submission was incurred during the months of April, May, June, July and November, 2018 (all Funds), August, September and October (SEF and TF). On the other hand, period of delay on other months significantly decreased from last year’s 83 to 236 days to this year’s 10 to 69 days.
observations.reason: Concerned officials did not provide explanation on their partial implementation of the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Local School Board to:
a. stop allocating and disbursing funds from SEF for purposes outside those authorized in the above mentioned Joint Circular in order not to jeopardize the achievement of the objectives of the Fund and to avoid disallowances in audit;
b. conform strictly with DepEd Order No. 59, s. 2016 in terms of who are those allowed to receive honoraria and the allowable amount thereof; and
c. require the concerned officials and employees to refund collected unauthorized honoraria immediately to the City Government.
observations.managementAction:
Management ensured that all disbursements out of SEF were authorized or allowed under the subject Joint Circular.
DepEd Order No. 59, s. 2016 had been complied with during the year.
As of December 31, 2018, the City has collected the amount of P225,000.00 from the refunds of concerned officials and employees.
observations.reason: Concerned officials did not provide explanation on their partial implementation of the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor direct:

a. the Inventory Committee to complete the inventory count of all properties
owned by the City to prove its existence and to submit the required report
thereon to the Auditor;

b. the City Accountant to provide details in the Property, Plant and Equipment
Ledger Cards (PPELCs) being maintained by their Office to provide adequate
information of the PPE; Make sure that PPELC and Real Property Cards per
category of PPE are properly maintained and updated;

¢. the General Services Officer (GSO) to make certain that Property Cards are
properly maintained, complete and updated to facilitate the physical count of
property and equipment;

d. the City Accountant and the GSO to continue their efforts to reconcile and
determine the nature of the discrepancies noted in the PPE accounts and make
the necessary adjustments/corrections, whenever applicable;

e the City GSO to prepare the updated Inventory and Inspection Report on
Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP), to account for all unserviceable and obsolete
items previously taken up in the books so as they may be properly disposed of and
dropped from the PPE accounts. Consequently, the disposal shall be made in
accordance with COA Circular No. 89-296.

We recommended that the City Accountant observe PPSAS 17 and initiate
efforts towards recognition in the books using cost or appraised value of unrecorded
PPE for fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements,

We recommended that the City Mayor require:

a. the GSO to consolidate weekly the RIS using the SSMI and submit to the
Accounting Office for recording of issued supplies and materials;

b. the City Accountant to compute the cost of supplies issued and ending inventory
based on the SSMI and to prepare a journal entry voucher (JEV) to record
expenditures using appropriate expenditures account; and

c. the GSO to conduct a complete physical count of all inventory items regularly at
the end of every semester and render a report thereon within the prescribed
deadline and reconcile the records of the GSO with the Accounting records on a
per category basis.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant and the
City Treasurer to closely monitor the cash balance of each fund and enjoin the City
Treasurer from certifying funds availability when no cash is available to avoid cash
deficits. Otherwise, appropriate sanctions maybe imposed against responsible
officials for existence of cash deficits.

We also recommended that trust fund be utilized only for the specific
purpose for which it was intended as required under Section 4(3) of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Health Officer and/or
other requisitioning medical personnel to ensure that every Purchase Request for
drugs and medicines shall be accompanied by a certification signed by the
requisitioning officer that the drug products being requisitioned fall within and
conform with PNDF, Volume I as required under EO No. 49 dated January 21, 1993
and DOH Administrative Order No. 2012-0023-A dated December 26, 2013.

We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor direct:

a. the General Services Officer and the created appraisal and disposal committee
to facilitate the immediate disposal of all unserviceable properties in order to
avail of a higher economic value when offered in public auction;

b. the concemed officials to strictly comply with the policies and procedures on the
disposal of property provided in COA Circular No. 92-386. Upon disposal, the
Accounting Office should prepare a Journal Entry Voucher to drop from the
books of accounts the sold/condemned properties:

c. the City Accountant to reclassify the unserviceable PPE accounts to Other Assets
account.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the fast-tracking of the Tax
Mapping operations to facilitate the cleansing of the Real Property Tax system in
coordination with the Offices of the Assessor and Treasurer to get rid of the
duplication in assessment of real property units and to adjust accordingly the
reported tax delinquencies. Consequently, direct the City Assessor to specify the
specific address/location of the properties to effect remedial measures to collect tax
delinquencies.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct the General
Services Office to ensure adequate control over the motor vehicles of the City by
performing the following control measures:

a. reconcile the Inventory Report of Transportation Equipment with Accounting
records;

b. immediately register in the name of the City the motor vehicles still registered in
the name of the previous owner;

c. make follow-up with the LTO regarding the motor vehicles with no plate
numbers;

d. exert effort to retrieve documents for those motor vehicles with no records on
file in the General Services Office;

e. observe annual registration of all vehicles; and

f. prepare the required Inventory and Inspection of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP)
for all motor vehicles found unserviceable to facilitate the disposal of the same.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:

a. submit immediately the 17 DVs covering disbursements amounting to
P3,088,807.90, otherwise the Audit Team will be constrained to issue a Notice of
Suspension for non-submission thereof; and

b. ensure that submission of financial reports/Disbursement Vouchers together
with the required supporting documents thereto conform strictly with the
deadline set in Section 7.2.1(a) of the RRSA prescribed in COA Circular
No. 2009-006.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Local School Board to:

a. stop allocating and disbursing funds from SEF for purposes outside those
authorized in the above mentioned Joint Circular in order not to jeopardize the
achievement of the objectives of the Fund and to avoid disallowances in audit;

b. conform strictly with the provisions of DepEd Order No. 59, s. 2016 in terms of
who are those allowed to receive honoraria and the allowable amount thereof; and

c. require the concerned officials and employees to refund immediately to the City
Government the unauthorized honoraria given to them.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Local School Board to see to it that SEF is utilized for purposes provided under Section 272 of Republic Act (RA) No. 7160, as implemented by DECS, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 01 dated April 14, 1998, to ensure that the purpose for which the fund was established is met.
observations.managementAction: No actions has been taken on the recommendation
observations.reason: Discussed in Part II, Observation No. 12

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct: the Inventory Committee to conduct a comprehensive physical inventory of all assets of the City of Bacoor to determine their actual existence and condition and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment which should incorporate the results of the physical count and not data copied from the Accounting Office records; the City Accountant to provide details in the PPE Ledger Cards being maintained by their Office to provide adequate information on the PPE; and the General Services Officer (GSO) to maintain Property Cards for individual property items owned by the City for reconciliation with accounting records and control purposes.
observations.managementAction:
The Inventory Committee physically counted the office furniture, fixtures and equipment in the Bacoor Government Center.
The Accounting Office maintained detailed records of PPE from 2005 onwards.
The General Services Officer (GSO) has started preparation of property card, however, it was prepared per department not by property items.
observations.reason:
The Inventory Committee was not able to conduct a comprehensive inventory-taking of all the PPE owned by the City Government of Bacoor due to the voluminous workload and limited manpower.
Discussed in Part II, Observation No. 1
The recorded lump-sum amounts with no available records which pertain to PPE acquired prior to 2005.
The GSO deemed it necessary to change the individual Property Cards and reconcile it with Property Tags attached to the respective properties.  There is delay in accomplishment due to volume of properties.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct: the City Accountant to see to it that what are recorded in the Land account are only those covered with perfected transactions and not mere appropriations for the purchase of lots.  She should ensure that valuation of Land is based on fair value for non-exchange transactions.
The General Services Officer, with the assistance of the City Assessor to identify all the land owned by the City Government and have them included in the Inventory of Land and taken up in the books of accounts.  They should establish which property should be prioritized in securing the land title and request of fund allocation for the purpose.
observations.managementAction:
The City Accountant has drawn JEV No. G-16-01-22 dated January 31, 2016 reversing the entry which booked up land valued at P15,000,000.00 which was based merely on appropriations.
The GSO had secured copies of all existing land titles and Deeds of Donations for the land owned by the City Government for proper identification, recording and basis for determining which property should be prioritized in securing land title and request of fund allocation for the purpose.

No action was taken on the recommendation.
No action was taken to implement the recommendation.
No action was taken to implement the recommendation.
Registrations of motor vehicles which are registered in the name of the City Government were regularly renewed.
The GSO was able to trace the whereabouts of motor vehicles not presented during inventory-taking.
The GSO had already prepared the required IIRUP for unserviceable motor vehicles for proper disposal thereof.
Some AREs for motor vehicles were renewed during the year.
The City motor pool has served the minor defects of motor vehicles.
observations.reason:
Voluminous quantities of properties owned by the City Government.
Discussed in Part II, Observation No. 9.
Inability to request for appropriation for transfer of registration of vehicles.
There is still unavailability of plates in the LTO.
Inability to obtain records from previous administrations.
Lack of adequate documents and records pertaining to motor vehicles acquired prior to 2013.
Inability to determine the persons responsible for some vehicles.
Negligence on the part of some drivers who are tasked to take care and be in-charge of the maintenance of the motor vehicles.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the General Services Office (GSO) to prepare the required Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) for all the unserviceable office furniture and equipment junked in the City motorpool so that disposal of the same could be effected and in order to avail of higher economic value when offered at public auction.
Upon disposal, the City Accountant should draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to drop from the books the unserviceable office furniture and equipment.
observations.managementAction:
The GSO prepared the required IIRUP for unserviceable motor vehicles, furniture and fixtures and equipment.
Upon disposal, the City Accountant should draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to drop from the books the unserviceable office furniture and equipment.
observations.reason:
Inability to prepare appraisal of the unserviceable properties.
Discussed in Part II, Observation No. 10
Disposal of unserviceable properties was not yet undertaken.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the implementation of the Tax Mapping Operations of the Assessor’s Office. Adequate funds, equipment, manpower and support should be appropriated and provided to the concerned Office for them to come up with a reliable Real Property Tax record that will establish a permanent link between the real properties in the field and the property assessment and tax records in the office.
observations.managementAction: The City Government through the City Assessor’s Office has started the process of cleansing the Real Property Tax List. A tax mapping program of more or less 125,000 RPUs is on going.
observations.reason: Discussed in Part II, Observation No. 6

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to: see to it that compromise agreements with taxpayers are covered with Sangguniang Panlungsod Ordinance wherein the specific guidelines in its implementation are duly stated for uniform and consistent application; require the submission of the Report of Collections and Deposits covering the Official Receipts issued in the settlement of Real Property Tax delinquencies under the installment basis; instruct the designated collectors in the City Treasurer’s Office to refrain from manually issuing Official Receipts for collection of taxes. All collections should be under the computerized system to facilitate verification.
observations.managementAction:
City Ordinance allowing compromise agreement and/or payment through installment basis of RPT embodying the rules and guidelines was enacted by the City Sanggunian during the year.
RCDs together with ORs issued in settlement of Real Property Tax delinquencies under installment basis were already submitted.
All ORs issued are already computerized.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct: the Requisitioning Department of the City to prepare Obligation Request based on commitments arising from contracts entered into by the City Government. They should ensure that supporting documents attached therein are valid, proper and legal; the City Budget Officer to check the completeness of the data in the Obligation Request and record the obligations in the registries as they are incurred; and the City Accountant to always see to it that no obligation shall be certified to Accounts Payable unless these are founded on a valid claim and properly supported by sufficient evidence, in compliance with Section 46 of PD No. 1177.
observations.managementAction:
The Budget Office already implemented the issuance of Obligation Requests (ObR) after the award of contract to the winning supplier/contractor to avoid blind certifications.
The City Budget Officer see to it that data in the ObR are complete and record the obligations in the registries as they are incurred.
The City Accountant is to always see to it that no obligation shall be certified to Accounts Payable unless these are founded on a valid claim and properly supported by sufficient evidence, in compliance with Section 46 of PD No. 1177.
observations.reason:
Discussed in Part II, Observation No. 5

Discussed in Part II, Observation No. 5

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct: the City Budget Officer to treat the unexpended balances of the 20% Development Fund, which were all Capital Outlay in nature, as Continuing Appropriations as provided in Sections 322 and 306 (d) and (e) of the Local Government Code; and the City Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) for the necessary accounting entries to adjust the affected accounts.  We further recommended that she refrain from treating unexpended balances of 20% Development Fund as Accounts Payable, instead consider the same as Continuing Appropriations.
observations.managementAction:
The City Budget Officer has properly observed treatment of the unexpended balances of the 20% Development Fund which are Capital Outlay in nature and treated it as Continuing Appropriations.
The City Accountant has drawn JEV No. G-16-03-04 dated March 31, 2016 adjusting the affected accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to: draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to adjust the amount set up for transfer to Trust Fund representing the unexpended balance of LDRRMF; and ensure the correctness of the Report on Utilization of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund by basing the utilization of LDRRMF on actual amount of expenditures stated in the Purchase Orders/Contracts and not on the amount in the Obligation Requests.
observations.managementAction:
In adherence to the recommendation, JEV No. G-16-03-05 dated March 31, 2016 was drawn by the City Accountant taking up into account the understated balance of LDRRMF amounting to P1,119,247.01.
The City Accountant taking up into account the understated balance of LDRRMF amounting to P1,119,247.01.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer and the City Social Welfare and Development Officer to: account for the difference of P1,757,554.00 worth of relief goods; and maintain Stock Cards to record the purchases and issuances of supplies, materials and relief goods procured out of LDRRMF.  Issuances of these items should be supported with Requisition Issue Slip and reported monthly to the Accounting Office thru the preparation and submission of Report of Supplies and Materials Issued (RSMI).
observations.managementAction:
The Management Official in-charge of the relief goods submitted the utilization report during the year.
Stock Cards are already prepared and maintained to record purchases and issuance of supplies and materials as well as relief goods. Issuances were supported with RIS.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct: a. the City Treasurer to consider the stock position and actual usage of accountable forms in requisitioning and purchasing the same to avoid overstocking and possible wastage; and b the City Treasurer to instruct the custodian of the accountable forms to accomplish properly the monthly Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms (RAAF) to serve as a tool in monitoring the balances of the forms.
observations.managementAction:
The City Treasurer has adhered with the recommendation of considering stock position and actual usage of accountable forms to prevent overstocking.
RAAFs were already prepared every month.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor ensure adequate assessment, documentation and screening in the hiring of consultants by undertaking the following: Conduct an assessment of whether the services are beyond the in-house capability of the City’s regular employees; Require the submission of credentials that would document the professional eligibility/expertise of the consultant for the services required of him/her; Execute the Terms of Reference (TOR) to serve as reference/basis for the expected output or deliverables of the consultants.
observations.managementAction:
The HRMO conducted assessment of the need to hire consultants.  No consultant was hired effective July 2016.
The HRMO has required the submission of credentials of each consultant hired during the period.
Management has executed the Terms of Reference (TOR) which served as reference for output of the employed consultants.
